competitors.label: MDN Arrow function expressions
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for arrow syntax, lexical this, and the missing constructor and prototype behavior.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on lexical this, arguments behavior, and the syntax rules of arrow functions.
theirGaps: It is less interview-shaped on the practical rule of when not to use arrows in object methods, constructors, and rebinding-heavy code.

realWorldUseCases: Arrow functions are concise, but the important production rule is when not to use them: object methods, constructors, and APIs that expect dynamic `this` or `arguments`.
actionableExamples: Arrow functions and `this` In regular functions, this depends on how the function is called. But in arrow functions, this is lexically bound — it refers to the value of this in the outer scope.
followUpConfusion: Be careful: - You can’t use arrow functions as constructors (new keyword will throw an error). - They also don’t have their own prototype.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains arrow semantics well, but our page is more explicit about the production rule developers actually need: when arrow functions are the wrong tool.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still have strong syntax examples, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is faster on the likely follow-up confusion around constructors, prototype absence, and dynamic this expectations.

actionableExamples: Add one short broken-object-method example later if we need more example distance over the canonical docs.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical reference for arrow functions, but our page is stronger for the interview prompt because it organizes the answer around the high-value production rule: when not to use arrows.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd arrow syntax in constructors
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep page for a practical arrow-function use case involving lexical this and constructor patterns.
theirStrengths: It is topic-direct on one of the most common practical arrow-function discussions: lexical this inside constructor-created methods.
theirGaps: It is narrower than the full interview prompt and less explicit on constructors, prototypes, and non-use cases outside that one pattern.

realWorldUseCases: Arrow functions are concise, but the important production rule is when not to use them: object methods, constructors, and APIs that expect dynamic `this` or `arguments`.
actionableExamples: Arrow functions and `this` In regular functions, this depends on how the function is called. But in arrow functions, this is lexically bound — it refers to the value of this in the outer scope.
followUpConfusion: Be careful: - You can’t use arrow functions as constructors (new keyword will throw an error). - They also don’t have their own prototype.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is directly relevant, but our page covers a wider set of production decisions instead of only one constructor-related use case.
actionableExamples: Its examples are practical and interview-friendly, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page answers more of the likely follow-up set in one place: lexical this, missing arguments, no constructor support, and no prototype.

actionableExamples: Keep the do-not-use cases close to the lexical-this explanation so the answer stays broader than one interview-prep pattern.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd gives a strong practical arrow-function angle, but our page is stronger because it covers the broader production rule set around lexical this, constructors, and prototype behavior.

competitors.label: javascript.info Arrow functions, the basics
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info is a topic-direct tutorial for arrow syntax, lexical this, and the trade-offs versus regular functions.
theirStrengths: It is strong on arrow syntax rules and lexical this behavior.
theirGaps: It is less compact as an interview answer and less explicit on the full set of non-use cases that cause production bugs.

realWorldUseCases: Arrow functions are concise, but the important production rule is when not to use them: object methods, constructors, and APIs that expect dynamic `this` or `arguments`.
actionableExamples: Arrow functions and `this` In regular functions, this depends on how the function is called. But in arrow functions, this is lexically bound — it refers to the value of this in the outer scope.
followUpConfusion: Be careful: - You can’t use arrow functions as constructors (new keyword will throw an error). - They also don’t have their own prototype.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is strong on the language behavior, but our page is more explicit about where arrow functions become the wrong choice in production code.
actionableExamples: Its syntax examples are strong and topic-direct, so the example criterion remains tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page pushes the follow-up further by bundling lexical this with the missing constructor, prototype, and arguments behavior in one answer.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one event-listener or object-method failure example to create more example separation.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is a strong arrow-functions tutorial, but our page is better calibrated for this interview prompt because it emphasizes the specific cases where arrows break expectations.
